result:
# The Museum of Almost

My grandmother collected moments that never quite happened. Not memories—she had plenty of those, stacked in her mind like cordwood—but the ghosts of possibilities. The almosts. The nearly-weres.

She kept them in mason jars.

I know how that sounds. But I was seven when she showed me her collection, and at seven, the world still makes a different kind of sense. Magic hasn't yet been educated out of you. The impossible is just another Tuesday.

"This one," she said, holding up a jar filled with what looked like fog, "is from 1952. Your grandfather almost kissed me at the county fair, right there by the Ferris wheel. But then his friend called out to him, and the moment passed."

"But he did kiss you eventually, right?" I asked. "You got married."

"Of course. But that first almost-kiss? That's something else entirely. It's pure potential. It holds all the nervousness, all the hope, all the wondering. The actual kiss was wonderful, but it was definite. It happened. This"—she swirled the jar gently—"this is still happening, in a way. Forever suspended."

She had hundreds of jars. They lined shelves in her basement, each one labeled in her careful cursive. "Almost bought a red dress, 1967." "Almost told Margaret the truth." "Almost moved to California." "Almost learned to play piano."

Some jars glowed faintly. Others seemed empty until you looked at them from the corner of your eye. A few hummed with an audible frequency, a tuning fork struck and sustaining.

"Don't you regret them?" I asked.

"Regret?" She laughed, a sound like wind chimes. "Sweetheart, these are my treasures. Every life is full of closed doors, but most people forget what was on the other side. I remember. I honor them."

"How do you catch them?"

She tapped her temple. "Attention. You have to notice when you're standing at a fork in the road, even if it's a tiny one. Most people walk past a thousand forks a day without seeing them. But if you pay attention, if you really *feel* that moment of choice, you can preserve it."

I thought she was wonderfully eccentric. A harmless oddity, like her collection of owl figurines or her insistence on wearing a hat to church.

Then I turned thirty-five.

I was cleaning out her house after the funeral when I found the empty jars on her workbench. Dozens of them, clean and waiting. And beside them, a note in her handwriting: "For you, when you're ready."

At thirty-five, I had a job I didn't hate but didn't love. A apartment that was fine. A dating life that was a graveyard of almosts. I'd almost gone to art school. Almost moved to Portland with Jamie. Almost quit my job to travel. Almost, almost, almost.

But I'd forgotten them. Let them dissolve like sugar in water, sweetening nothing.

I picked up a jar, feeling foolish. Closed my eyes. Tried to remember.

Art school. I'd been accepted. I'd held the letter in my hands, felt the weight of it. Felt the terror and exhilaration. I'd stood at my bedroom window for an hour, just holding that letter, being someone who might become an artist. Then I'd chosen the practical path. State school. Business degree. Sensible.

My hands warmed. The jar grew heavy.

When I opened my eyes, there was something inside. Not fog—more like a shimmer, like heat rising from summer pavement. And inside that shimmer, I could see myself at eighteen, holding that letter, infinite.

I sat on the basement floor and cried.

Over the next year, I collected my almosts. The job I didn't apply for. The person I didn't speak to on the train who was reading my favorite book. The dance class I bookmarked but never joined. The novel I outlined but never wrote.

My collection grew.

People would think I was dwelling on regrets, but they'd be wrong. Each jar wasn't a monument to failure—it was proof that I'd stood at crossroads. That I'd had choices. That my life was full of possibility, even the possibilities I didn't choose.

The almost-kiss jar, though—that one was different.

There's someone at my coffee shop. We've been doing this dance for six months. Lingering glances. Careful smiles. Small talk that wants to be bigger talk. Yesterday, we reached for the sugar at the same time, and our hands touched, and the air between us crystallized into something I could almost taste.

I could bottle it. I could preserve this perfect, terrifying potential forever.

Or.

I left the empty jar at home today.

When I see them, I'm going to say hello. I'm going to ask their name. I'm going to risk the deflation of the actual, the way reality never quite matches the shimmer of the possible.

But here's what I've learned from my grandmother's museum: The almosts are beautiful, but they're not alive. They don't breathe. They don't grow. They're perfect because they're preserved, and they're preserved because they're dead.

I want something alive.

I want to stand at the fork in the road, acknowledge it, honor it—and then choose. Not perfectly. Not without fear. But choose.

Tonight, I'll add one more jar to my collection, and I'll label it carefully: "Almost kept playing it safe. Didn't."

That almost might be my favorite one yet.
